lesson.menu_title: Handling Difficult Conversations -- Giving and Receiving Feedback
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 -- Handling Difficult Conversations -- Feedback
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Natalie Viegas | Aula 10 -- Handling Difficult Conversations

listenings.text: Hi Natalie, this is David from the leadership team. I would like to schedule your quarterly review for Friday at three. Before we meet, please prepare some honest feedback on the last project. I want this to be a calm, constructive conversation, not a list of complaints. When you receive feedback, try not to be defensive; just acknowledge the valid points and ask questions. When you give feedback, be tactful and specific. Tell the person what worked well first, then address one issue at a time. If someone underperformed, do not attack them; reassure them and agree on a clear next step. Remember, good feedback builds accountability and trust. Please send me three short notes before Friday. Thank you, and see you then.

Hi, this is Natalie, recording a quick reflection after my review with David. He said that my work on the last project was strong, and that the client was very happy. However, he told me that I sometimes take feedback in a defensive way. At first I wanted to explain myself, but I stopped and just listened. I acknowledged that he had a valid point. He asked me to pause before I answer, and to address one issue at a time. He also reassured me that the goal is to grow, not to criticize. I told him that I would work on it. It was a difficult conversation, but a constructive one. I think I handled it well, and I feel more accountable now.

extra_audio.text: First, you start with something positive and specific that worked well. Then you address one issue at a time, not everything at once. After that, you stay calm and tactful, so the other person does not feel attacked. Next, you listen and acknowledge any valid points the person makes. Finally, you agree on a clear next step and reassure the person about the goal.
